posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
These are always so darned interesting to read - and so much, to me, has to do with whether you bow down at the altar of ‘typicity’ or not. There are lots of great expressions of Grenache around the world, and there are lots of great wines made with the Grenache grape that don’t necessarily remind me of ‘grenache’ that are heavily sought after, and many have been mentioned here.

I’ve been fortunate to take part in a few epic Grenache tastings featuring top Spanish, Australian, American and French grenaches, with many folks insisting that it’s ‘okay’ to include wines that contain a percentage of something else in them (at which point I always ask whether the same could be done with a top Pinot tasting, at which everyone, of course, says hell no).

To me, the purity and ethereal nature of Grenache is best expressed by Rayas, though I’ve only had the chance to try it on a few occasions (and the Pignans that I’ve had have not been far behind).

Cheers.

I don’t like fumy, bitter ETOH in my wine but I haven’t found much of a correlation between that character and the ABV on the label.

I’ll join the chorus on CdP and southern Rhone as best for average and peak levels of Grenache quality imho. Best grenache I ever had were some Albans in the early 00s but I don’t follow them. That said I think Grenache is one of CAs least successful grape varieties overall.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I never understood the obsession over ABV. I pretty much ignore the friggin’ number on the label and judge the wine on its own merits in the glass. I don’t like fumy, bitter ETOH in my wine but I haven’t found much of a correlation between that character and the ABV on the label.

The correlation is to your palate, not to others. For many, like me, elevated alcohol levels are like scratching nails on a chalk board. It’s largely why the modernization of CDP - heavily influenced by Cambie and Parker, and of course other sycophantic critics - came close to killing this once glorious region for me. I drink very few CDP producers these days. It used to be one of the more significant regions in my purchasing. Labels are important. If a Southern Rhone says 15% or more, I generally avoid it. Sure, I could be missing a decent one here or there, but I’ve drank far too many Rhones to know what generally works for me and what doesn’t. I’m not going to drop $75 or more on a CDP (or $800+ on a Rayas) at 15%+ just hoping it works.

Cheers.

California is definitely up there. I think grenache in California is far more suitable across the state as a whole than any other varietal. It grows great everywhere and produces distinct wines in each part of the state… and Im a Pinot guy saying this.

Grenache is justa lovely two-sided coin of expression. Not my Rayas!

And you believe the label??? I’ve run alcohols on many CdPs. Some claim 13.5 and are 16+%. I can guarantee you that your Rayas wines are north of 15%. It’s the nature of Grenache, despite what the label says.

It doesn’t matter what the alcohol level is, although I’d be surprised if you tested Rayas, and discovered it was that high, (much over 15% or even close to 16%) Grenache is a high-alcohol grape.

Rayas has one of the coolest terroir’s in Chateauneuf and it’s soils are pure, humid sand.

Regardless, Rayas never tastes overripe or feels hot. Including 2003, 2007 etc. There is a beautiful sense of purity in the fruit other estates are not able to produce.

Count me in

In the verticale - report by Pierre Citerne :
10. Châteauneuf-du-Pape : Château Rayas 1995
DS18/18,5+ – PC18/18,5 – LG18+ – PR18/19 – MF18,5/19. Note moyenne : 18,5
La robe annonce un changement de registre radical. Très jeune et brillante, rubis sombre sans reflets orangés, noire en son centre, elle étonne autant qu’elle impressionne.
Comme la robe, le nez est profond et monolithique, imposant plus que captivant. La présence fruitée est énorme, confite mais leste et distinguée, un fruit terrien, sombre (on pense à la mûre, à la myrtille, un peu comme dans un vintage portugais où un Maury…) ; les notes de cèdre et de truffe qui émergent à l’aération sont magnifiques mais ne parviennent pas à dissiper l’impression générale d’austérité et de quant à soi.
En bouche le volume du vin est considérable, son extrait sec impressionnant, avec une présence acide au premier plan. On est effectivement rentré dans un autre registre, celui de la monumentalité, avec un vin (il y en aura d’autres dans la série) qui évoque par son amplitude, sa compacité (paradoxalement…), sa « noirceur » et son indifférence au flux du temps certains hermitages (celui de Chave ou La Chapelle en 1978 par exemple).

posts.name: Greg Kramer
posts.cooked:
The correlation is to your palate, not to others. For many, like me, elevated alcohol levels are like scratching nails on a chalk board. It’s largely why the modernization of CDP - heavily influenced by Cambie and Parker, and of course other sycophantic critics - came close to killing this once glorious region for me. I drink very few CDP producers these days. It used to be one of the more significant regions in my purchasing. Labels are important. If a Southern Rhone says 15% or more, I generally avoid it. Sure, I could be missing a decent one here or there, but I’ve drank far too many Rhones to know what generally works for me and what doesn’t. I’m not going to drop $75 or more on a CDP (or $800+ on a Rayas) at 15%+ just hoping it works.

I similarly avoided high alcohol wines and railed against producers amping up their wines to chase points and $. Two experiences recalibrated my thinking (i’m not naming names because I sell wine from both referenced producers):

  1. Cabernet Franc in Bourgueil from a staunchly traditional producer. I shunned their 2018’s for quite some time, even though the winemaker told me it is one of his greatest vintages in his 32 years making wine. The ABV’s were 14.5-14.9 compared with mid 13’s the prior years. Finally, I tasted. While bigger they are impeccably balanced with no heat. I think they will age well and become stellar wines.

  2. A new producer from Rioja Baja who largely eschews Tempranillo in favor of Garnacha and Mazuelo, which he considers the true indigenous varieties and better suited to his vineyards. The Garnacha is old vines grown at very high elevations. His 'regular" cuvee is 15% and his small production, single vineyard (planted 125 years ago - 85% original rootstock - at the highest elevation allowed clocks in at 15.5%. Again, no trace of heat and they are not only balanced but, indeed, elegant.

So, there are big wines and small wines but the key is balance not ABV - assuming one enjoys both sizes.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I like Rayas. I’ve had it in about 15 vintages, from 4 - 20+ years of age. I don’t remotely get it as the pinnacle of Grenache. I’d easily put it in the top 10, maybe top 5 of those I’ve had.

Beaucastel simply isn’t Grenache. Unless I’m mistaken, it is majority Mourvedre. Calling it ‘Grenache’ is like calling Haut-Brion ‘Cabernet Sauvignon’, when it usually has more Merlot than Cabernet.

to Laurent Gibet: I’ve enjoyed your posts, but must respectfully disagree with Terroir al Limit ‘Les Manyes’. I’ve only had two vintages, with the similar notes for both. This is relatively low alcohol but heavy, weird, spoofilated putrid swill. I once had an earlier vintage in a blind tasting with Robert Parker. He rated it last of 10 top wines based on Grenache. When I told him that his publication had rated it 98, he got an extremely sour look on his face (he was not the person who rated it, obviously).

Dan Kravitz

Well, let’s be clear, my CDP knowledge is not on par with yours, love reading your CDP comments. I get what you are saying, and that change has directly correlated to CDP becoming a substantially smaller component of my cellar than it was just 15 years ago. My daily drinkers used to be 2x or more per week CDRs and CDP perhaps 2X per month. Now I just stick to some key producers and do not generally buy the uber-ripe vintages. I’m a bit limited here in my office, with it only having a smaller fridge, but in looking at the CDPs and Gigondas I have in there - younger, as I received wines in my mailing room then slowly move them to storage, the 2015 Beaucastel, 2016 Beaucastel, 2017 Pegau and 2016 Cayron are between 14-14.5%. While sure that means with labeling variances these could be a bit higher, or even lower, that still likely keeps them lower than CDPs that already come out labeled at 15-15.5%.

Beaucastel really isn’t Grenache given that there is usually as much if not more Mourvedre in the mix. I haven’t kept up with Cayron, only trying them again last year, after a long hiatus. All your Pegaus since 01 or 03 (I’m not sure which) will show 14% alcohol. Before that, they all showed 13.5%. I love Pegau for hands off winemaking. But they also seem to be hands off with their labeling. I don’t know what their alcohol content is, but it surely isn’t that, year after year. As I said, Charvin hits 15% in solar years. So do lots of other non-Cambie winemakers. If you pick your grapes when they taste ripe and not by when the laboratory tells you, you take what the weather gives you.

On the business of Rayas buying Charvin grapes before they bottled their own wine, I remember Laurent telling me that, like everybody else, they sold off their wine to negociants, probably Guigal, which paid top dollar for the best wines and thus made a pretty good CdP back in the 80s. I never heard of Rayas buying anybody else’s grapes. It’s a little more likely that some vineyard switched hands, but, again, not very that Rayas would either sell parts of its CdP vineyard or buy a non-Pignan wine to blend in, given that the quality of the wine is very much bound up with the vineyard. But I don’t have any factual knowledge to say with certainty that some such thing didn’t happen.

I guess I like different qualities in Grenache than most of you guys. Have not been particularly impressed by any Grenache from Rhône, because they tend to be too ripe, alcoholic, soft and sweet for my taste with often all too low acidity. Usually the lower the Grenache portion is in the blend, the better.

Hard to say any producer (and I’ve not had any Rayas), but when it comes to regions, I think the high-altitude Garnachas from Madrid are the best expressions of the variety I can think of. Almost everywhere else Grenache is way too dull and blowzy for my taste, whereas many Madrid Garnachas have been wonderfully fresh, pure and precise with a beautifully Burgundian overall feel. Admittedly, the alcohol levels can get quite high even in those wines - because tiger can’t lose its stripes - but despite this they can be some of the most alluring examples of the variety I know.

There are some very impressive wines made in Priorat, but can’t think of many where Grenache has been the dominant grape variety. On the contrary, I think the higher the Garnacha portion is in Priorat wines, the less inclined I’m to like it.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: I would describe the commonality between des Tours and Rayas differently. None of the wines taste like Rayas (as I have said above, though, I only have a little experience with Rayas) any more than Fonsalette or Fonsalette Syrah tastes like Rayas. The wines come from different terroirs and have different blends. And, of course, none of them are 100% Grenache. But they are identifiably made by the same hand. I would have said that about des Tours before 1997, though, so I should say by identifiably the same school of winemaking (in the sense in which, say, a painting by a Rembrandt apprentice comes from Rembrandt’s studio).

It is pretty profound, but I think many of the older Louis Reynaud-era wines can be as good or better. Rayas from the '50s and '60s might be my desert island red wine (and I write this as someone who lives in Burgundy for much of the year). Jacques Reynaud’s wines were a bit more muscular and extracted: the 1995 is still opaque and impenetrable, and the 1989, my vintage, is still just at the beginning of its interesting life. My impression is that the Louis Reynaud wines were a bit more elegant: just as concentrated, but a bit less blocky and chunky if that makes sense. It will be interesting to see what Emmanuel’s wines do with 40 years on the clock…

Regarding Rayas, this video is pretty exceptional for Francophone readers… https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Vqqg_NRLOEs If I did not have too many things on my plate I’d try to add English subtitles, but for a 26-minute video that’s quite an undertaking. You will note that Reynaud uses whole bunches but crushes the fruit very thoroughly, and that fermentations take place with a submerged cap plus pump-overs… I wonder how much techniques have evolved since Louis’ and Jacques’ eras?

Allow me to point here what Pierre Citerne (who is now journalist at La Revue du Vin de France) reported for the group after a fantastic verticale in our club in Toulouse :
…
Malgré cette unicité de Rayas, et la constance de son unicité au fil des millésimes, il nous a semblé qu’une césure stylistique pouvait être perçue entre la période où le vin était produit par Jacques Reynaud (jusqu’en 1996) et ce qui a suivi. Les vins de Jacques Reynaud possèdent souvent un caractère massif, une densité, une monumentalité (1995, 1989, 1985…) que n’ont pas les millésimes plus récents ; ce style peut sembler en fin de compte assez proche des plus grandes réussites d’autres producteurs « traditionnalistes » de Châteauneuf (Henri Bonneau ou la famille Féraud par exemple). Les millésimes cités semblent encore loin de leur apogée. Les vins d’Emmanuel Reynaud, qui a pris la succession de son oncle, semblent vouloir atteindre à davantage de finesse, d’élégance, de précision dans la définition du type Rayas. De vins peut-être plus aériens, moins chargés, moins imposants (pour l’instant… Rayas peut se densifier avec l’âge, nous avons pu le vérifier plusieurs fois), qui s’éloignent encore davantage de Châteauneuf pour affirmer plus haut l’unicité du cru, un vin « autre », comme un Musigny du Soleil… Peut-être plus apollinien que dionysiaque (en tout cas un vin de caractère gréco-latin, ce qui pourrait excuser en partie la comparaison mythologique éculée…) Je suis personnellement très heureux, après avoir entendu des années durant d’assez nombreuses critiques négatives concernant le style d’Emmanuel Reynaud (critiques qui m’ont toujours semblé fondées davantage sur le syndrome du « c’était mieux avant » que sur une observation objective de l’évolution stylistique du vin), que la sensibilité et la justesse de son approche soient enfin largement reconnues.
…

Les vins de Jacques Reynaud possèdent souvent un caractère massif, une densité, une monumentalité (1995, 1989, 1985…) que n’ont pas les millésimes plus récents ; ce style peut sembler en fin de compte assez proche des plus grandes réussites d’autres producteurs « traditionnalistes » de Châteauneuf (Henri Bonneau ou la famille Féraud par exemple). Les vins d’Emmanuel Reynaud, qui a pris la succession de son oncle, semblent vouloir atteindre à davantage de finesse, d’élégance, de précision dans la définition du type Rayas. De vins peut-être plus aériens, moins chargés, moins imposants (pour l’instant… Rayas peut se densifier avec l’âge, nous avons pu le vérifier plusieurs fois), qui s’éloignent encore davantage de Châteauneuf pour affirmer plus haut l’unicité du cru, un vin « autre », comme un Musigny du Soleil… Peut-être plus apollinien que dionysiaque (en tout cas un vin de caractère gréco-latin, ce qui pourrait excuser en partie la comparaison mythologique éculée…) Je suis personnellement très heureux, après avoir entendu des années durant d’assez nombreuses critiques négatives concernant le style d’Emmanuel Reynaud (critiques qui m’ont toujours semblé fondées davantage sur le syndrome du « c’était mieux avant » que sur une observation objective de l’évolution stylistique du vin), que la sensibilité et la justesse de son approche soient enfin largement reconnues.
…

Interesting reflections (and also illustrative of the greater freedom French wine writers have: if I declared that Emmanuel’s vintages of Rayas are “more Apollonian than Dionysian, but in any case Greco-Roman in character, which may to some extent excuse this tired mythological analogy”, I would find myself in John’s Bingo thread pretty rapidly I think).

I agree with quite a bit of it, though I do think Jacques Reynaud’s Rayas were still pretty unique wines, and I don’t think they would be easy to confuse with Bonneau or Pégaü blind. For me, rather than approximating more to some sort of idealized typology of what Rayas “is”, I just wonder if Emmanuel’s wines might be closer in style to the Louis Reynaud-era wines? But it’s hard to say. I reckon they have a bit more overt “sucrosité” of fruit than those wines did, but of course I never tried Louis Reynaud Rayas when it was young.

Haha that works! The first time I went, with a few friends from my student tasting group, we arrived early and waited outside. At the hour of our appointment, an upstairs window opened and a head with a flat cap on briefly looked out. 15 minutes later, it looked out again, and then Reynaud must have decided we had passed the first test and came down to open the door. His line was “ah, les débutants d’Oxford sont arrivés”.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The 2000 Rayas I had earlier this year was the best Grenache I have ever tasted. So Rayas. I do have a soft spot for Outpost’s Grenache, though.

This is positively interesting, because the 2000 Rayas has long and often being called weak (e.g. by RMP) which I don´t agree with; it is very bright in colour but also very intense, sweet and complex - and fully mature imo

I think it went through a sort of exaggeratedly tertiary, almost oxidative, phase around age 10-15, before bouncing back. It now tastes younger than it did a decade ago. The 2001 did something similar. Not sure what was going on or why, but I have found more recent vintages to be developing in a less idiosyncratic fashion.
